s: Renzo Piano's quiet masterpiece — 25,000 works, barely any on the walls
w: Dominique de Menil fled Nazi Paris in 1941 with her oil-fortune husband and a Dominican friar who told them to spend it on art. Renzo Piano's 1987 building filters Texas light through curved white 'leaves' so nothing bleaches. Only around 5% of the 25,000-object collection is on view at once. Free, forever, by design.
_facts: John and Dominique de Menil fled Nazi-occupied France in 1941, settling in Houston
Opened June 1987, designed by Renzo Piano
Over 25,000 works in the permanent collection; only ~5% displayed at any time
Natural light filtered through custom ferro-cement 'leaf' louvers on the roof
Free admission by the founders' original stipulation

s: The Eighth Wonder of the World — closed since 2009, refusing to die
w: When it opened in April 1965, they called it the Eighth Wonder of the World, and it earned the title: first domed multipurpose stadium, first air-conditioned arena, first artificial turf — grass died in the dim interior, so they invented AstroTurf. The Astros and Oilers left; so did the Rockets. It closed in 2009. Enormous and unbowed, still waiting.
_facts: Opened April 9, 1965 — first domed multi-purpose stadium in the world
Grass died without sunlight; AstroTurf invented here in 1966
First animated scoreboard ('Astrolite') with thousands of light bulbs
Closed to public since 2009, used as storage for NRG Park
2024 plan: convert to hotel, arena, retail and elevated boulevard

s: Philip Johnson's optical-illusion towers that ended the glass-box era
w: Two dark-bronze trapezoidal towers, 36 storeys each, separated by a ten-foot gap and capped by a 115-foot glass pyramid atrium. Johnson designed them as a deliberate optical illusion: walk around the block and they morph. Completed 1976, they broke the Miesian glass-box orthodoxy and launched postmodernism. He picked up the first-ever Pritzker Prize partly for this.
_facts: Completed 1976, designed by Philip Johnson and John Burgee
Two 36-storey trapezoidal towers separated by a 10-foot gap
Glass pyramid atrium 115 feet tall covers the gap at street level
Deliberately designed as an optical illusion — appearance changes with viewpoint
Credited with 'breaking the modernist glass box' and introducing postmodernism
Philip Johnson won 1978 AIA Gold Medal and first Pritzker Prize (1979) partly for this work

s: A horseshoe of crashing water, 64 feet tall, beside a Houston freeway
w: The Williams Tower next door is 64 storeys, so Johnson built this horseshoe waterfall to match — 64 feet of St Joe brick and limestone, water crashing on both the concave and convex faces, 78,500 gallons recycled every three hours. Completed 1985, ringed by 118 live oaks. In a city that bakes, standing inside it is the best free cooling available.
_facts: Designed by Philip Johnson / John Burgee, operational from 1985
64 feet tall to symbolise the 64 storeys of the adjacent Williams Tower
Water flows on both concave and convex faces simultaneously
78,500 gallons total, recycled every 3 hours 2 minutes
Built from St Joe brick and Indiana Buss limestone with black granite base
118 Texas live oak trees surround the structure

s: 'Home of Happy Feet' — Third Ward's Art Moderne jazz palace, back since 2023
w: Anna and Charles Dupree opened this white-brick Art Moderne ballroom in 1939 above five storefronts on Elgin Street, and it became the pulse of Black Houston's music scene through the Jim Crow decades. It closed around 1972. A $9.7 million restoration by Project Row Houses, partly funded by the African American Cultural Heritage Action Fund, brought it back in March 2023. The sign is back.
_facts: Opened 1939, designed by architect Lenard Gabert in Art Moderne style
Founded by Anna and Charles Dupree; slogan 'Home of Happy Feet'
Central to Black Houston music scene through Jim Crow era
Closed around 1972; fell into decades of neglect
$9.7 million restoration; reopened March 30, 2023
Partly funded by $3 million grant from African American Cultural Heritage Action Fund

s: Bought for $800 in 1872 so freed people could celebrate Juneteenth
w: Four Black community leaders pooled $800 in 1872 to buy ten acres so formerly enslaved Houstonians had somewhere to mark Juneteenth — the city wasn't going to provide one. It became Houston's first park and, under Jim Crow, its only public park open to African Americans. A $33 million renovation completed in 2017 revived the celebrations. Texas made Juneteenth a federal holiday 49 years later.
_facts: Purchased 1872 by Richard Allen, Richard Brock, Jack Yates and Elias Dibble for $800
Oldest park in Houston and the oldest in Texas
Under Jim Crow (1922-1940) it was the sole public park available to African Americans
Juneteenth celebrations ceased 2007; revived after $33 million renovation (completed 2017)

s: Ima Hogg's mansion: Texas's First Lady gave it all away, name and all
w: Her father Jim Hogg was Texas's first native-born governor; her name came from a character in his brother's Civil War poem. She spent her life insisting she never had a sister called Ura. In 1957 she donated this 1928 Neoclassical mansion — and 4,700 American decorative art objects — to the Museum of Fine Arts. The architect John Staub was ordered to sacrifice exactly one tree. He did.
_facts: House designed by John F. Staub, built 1927-28 in Neoclassical Revival / English Regency styles
Ima Hogg (1882-1975) donated it 1957 to MFAH; opened to public 1966
~4,700 American decorative art objects, 1620-1876, displayed in 28 period rooms
Ima's name came from a character in uncle Thomas Hogg's Civil War poem 'The Fate of Marvin'
Legend of a sister named 'Ura Hogg' is false; Ima denied it throughout her life

s: The 'Garage Mahal' — outsider art on four wheels, Houston's proudest export
w: Ann Harithas and James Harithas — she an artist, he former Corcoran director — opened this shrine to decorated vehicles on Heights Boulevard in 1998, calling it the Garage Mahal. Houston's art-car culture grew from a 1984 auction of a painted Ford station wagon called the Fruitmobile; the Orange Show Foundation turned that into the world's largest art car parade. The museum was the permanent home. Closed 2024, relocating to The Orange Show.
_facts: Opened February 1998 at 140 Heights Blvd; nicknamed the 'Garage Mahal'
Founded by Ann Harithas and James Harithas (former director of the Corcoran Gallery)
Houston art car culture traces to a 1984 Orange Show Foundation auction of the 'Fruitmobile'
First art car parade: 11 vehicles on Montrose Boulevard in 1986
Closed April 2024; planned relocation to The Orange Show complex

s: Texas's oldest craft brewery in a 1914 schoolboard warehouse, Lyons Avenue
w: Rice graduates Brock Wagner and Kevin Bartol named their brewery after the 7th-century bishop who is patron saint of brewers, and started it in 1994. In 2010 they spent $6 million on a three-storey 1914 brick building that used to feed Houston schoolchildren — the HISD food service warehouse. Beer garden and restaurant added 2018. Saturday tours packed. Texas's oldest craft brewery.
_facts: Founded 1994 by Brock Wagner and Kevin Bartol, Rice University graduates
Named for Saint Arnulf of Metz, patron saint of brewing
Texas's oldest craft brewery
Moved to 1914 brick building (former HISD food service warehouse) in 2010 after $6 million redevelopment
Beer garden, taproom and restaurant added in 2018

s: The world's largest medical city — one surgery every three minutes
w: Founded in 1945 on 2.1 square miles south of the Museum District, the Texas Medical Center is the largest medical complex on earth: 60-plus institutions, 21 hospitals, four medical schools, over 106,000 employees. It contains the world's largest children's hospital and the world's largest cancer hospital. One surgery every three minutes. Houston had no obvious reason to become a global medical capital. It just decided to.
_facts: Founded 1945, partly funded by the M.D. Anderson Foundation
Largest medical center in the world; 2.1-square-mile campus
Over 60 institutions, 21 hospitals, 4 medical schools, 106,000+ employees
Houses world's largest children's hospital (Texas Children's) and cancer hospital (MD Anderson)
Performs one surgery every 3 minutes; one birth every 20 minutes annually

s: Freed people settled this swamp and paved its streets with hand-made bricks
w: In 1866 about a thousand formerly enslaved Houstonians settled flood-prone scrubland along Buffalo Bayou because it was cheap and white Houstonians didn't want it. By 1900 they had built a neighbourhood of doctors, lawyers and tradespeople — and paved at least some streets with bricks they made themselves after city officials refused to. By 2012, only 30 of 563 original buildings survived. The brick streets endure.
_facts: Settled from 1866 by ~1,000 formerly enslaved people on land too swampy for white buyers
By late 19th c, community included blacksmiths, doctors, lawyers, teachers
Streets paved with hand-made bricks after city officials refused to do so
Carnegie Colored Library (1912) demolished 1962 for Interstate 45 construction
By 2012, only 30 of original 563 historic buildings survived gentrification

s: Houston's oldest congregation, founded when Texas was still a republic
w: The congregation was founded in 1839 when Texas was still an independent republic — first services were in the Capitol Building. The current Late Gothic Revival structure dates to 1893, designed by Silas McBee, and became the diocesan cathedral in 1949. In a 1938 fire, firefighters saved the carved rood screen. The church went on to seed dozens of Houston's Episcopal parishes as missions.
_facts: Congregation founded 1839, when Texas was an independent republic
First chartered congregation in Houston; early services held in the Capitol Building
Current building 1893, designed by Silas McBee in Late Gothic Revival style
Became Episcopal Diocese of Texas cathedral in 1949
Rood screen survived a 1938 fire due to firefighters' intervention

s: A 1911 Beaux Arts terminal absorbed whole into a retractable-roof ballpark
w: Houston Union Station opened in 1911 in granite, limestone and terracotta, its marble interiors connecting the city to the national rail network. When the city built Minute Maid Park (now Daikin Park) in 2000 — one of MLB's first retractable-roof stadiums — the old terminal stayed: its grand concourse is the main entrance; a working miniature train now runs the left-field wall.
_facts: Houston Union Station built 1911; granite, limestone, terracotta exterior; marble interiors
Incorporated into Minute Maid Park (now Daikin Park) when it opened March 30, 2000
One of MLB's first retractable-roof stadiums
Station's main concourse now serves as the park's primary entrance
A working model train runs along the left field wall
'Tal's Hill' — 90-foot centre-field slope with in-play flagpole — removed after 2016 season

s: Joel Osteen's 16,800-seat megachurch in the old Houston Rockets arena
w: John Osteen founded a Baptist congregation in 1959; son Joel became pastor after John's death in January 1999. In 2005 the church bought the Compaq Center — the 16,800-seat arena where the Houston Rockets played for 29 years — and spent $95 million converting it. Six services a week, English and Spanish, averaging 45,000 weekly attendees: the largest congregation in the United States. God moved in July 16, 2005.
_facts: Founded May 10, 1959 by John Osteen as a Baptist church
Joel Osteen became senior pastor October 1999, after his father's death
Moved to former Compaq Center (Houston Rockets arena) July 16, 2005
Compaq Center held 16,800 — Houston Rockets home for 29 years
Averages ~45,000 weekly attendees across 4 English and 2 Spanish services
Largest congregation in the United States

s: Isamu Noguchi's outdoor rooms: Matisse and Calder between the live oaks
w: Noguchi designed this half-acre garden beside the Museum of Fine Arts in 1986 as a series of outdoor rooms where 25 sculptures share Southern light and Texas live oaks: Matisse, Calder, Louise Bourgeois, Frank Stella, and Ellsworth Kelly's site-specific Houston Triptych. Texans Jim Love and Joseph Havel too. Free, open daily, easy to miss between the museum buildings — which is a feature, not a fault.
_facts: Designed by artist and landscape architect Isamu Noguchi; opened 1986
25 sculptures from the MFAH collection including Matisse, Calder, Louise Bourgeois, Frank Stella
Ellsworth Kelly's Houston Triptych is site-specific
Texas artists include Jim Love and Joseph Havel
Free admission; open daily dawn to dusk

s: Founded on a murdered man's fortune, free of tuition for 53 years
w: William Marsh Rice was chloroformed in his sleep in September 1900 by his own lawyer and butler, who had forged a new will. The lawyer was convicted of murder; the butler turned state's evidence. Rice's estate — $4.6 million, worth over $125 million today — funded the university he had planned. It opened on September 23, 1912, the 12th anniversary of his murder. Per the original charter: no tuition. It stayed free until 1965.
_facts: Founder William Marsh Rice murdered by chloroform September 23, 1900, by lawyer Albert Patrick and butler Charles Jones
Patrick convicted of murder 1901, pardoned 1912; butler testified against him
Estate $4.6 million (c.$125 million today) endowed the university
Opened September 23, 1912 — 12th anniversary of Rice's murder — with 59 students
Per original charter, tuition was free until 1965
Campus is Byzantine Revival: sand-pink brick, large archways and columns

s: Up to 250,000 Mexican free-tailed bats spiral out of a highway bridge at dusk
w: Mexican free-tailed bats — kin to Austin's famous Congress Avenue colony — pack the expansion joints of the Waugh Drive bridge over Buffalo Bayou. The colony numbers in the hundreds of thousands. Every evening from spring through autumn they spiral out in a dark column above the water, the emergence taking 20 minutes to clear. The Buffalo Bayou Partnership put a watching platform underneath. It smells of guano. It is magnificent.
_facts: Mexican free-tailed bats (Tadarida brasiliensis) roost in Waugh Drive bridge expansion joints
Colony estimated at hundreds of thousands, commonly cited as up to 250,000
Emerges in a spiral column at dusk; takes approximately 20 minutes to clear
Buffalo Bayou Partnership installed a dedicated bat-watching platform beneath the bridge
Best viewing: spring through autumn at sunset

s: A 132-acre global garden opened in 2020 on a former municipal golf course
w: The city had a golf course in Park Place along Sims Bayou. Houstonians decided the city needed a botanic garden more than a public links, and the Glenbrook Golf Course became 132 acres of living collection: a Global Collection Garden drawing on Mediterranean climates, East Asia and the American Southwest; a Culinary Garden; children's discovery trails. Opened 2020, the newest major public garden in the United States.
_facts: Opened 2020 on former Glenbrook Golf Course site along Sims Bayou
132 acres; Global Collection Garden, Culinary Garden, seasonal events
Non-profit member of American Public Gardens Association and Botanic Gardens Conservation International
Located in Park Place neighborhood, Southeast Houston
